---
title: "v3 Phase E: validation upgrades"
subtitle: "Calibration audit, capture rates, Platt vs isotonic, SHAP recipe, leakage audit, block-size diagnostic"
author: "Sky King"
date: 2026-06-02
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
execute:
  warning: false
---

## What this notebook is

v3 Phase E added six diagnostic audits. Each one will run automatically as
part of the Phase F.1 retrain pipeline. This notebook shows the empirical
findings on the v2-killed data we already have on disk.

## E.6 spatial block-size empirical diagnostic

The v2 plan inherited a 20 km block size for spatial CV without empirical
justification. E.6 runs a pre-flight diagnostic at 10 / 15 / 20 / 25 / 30 km
and reports the trade-off: smaller blocks have more folds but more
adjacency leakage; larger blocks lose statistical power but tighten the CV.

```{python}
#| label: block-size-diag
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

REPO_ROOT = Path.cwd()
while not (REPO_ROOT / "pyproject.toml").exists() and REPO_ROOT.parent != REPO_ROOT:
    REPO_ROOT = REPO_ROOT.parent

bs = pd.read_csv(REPO_ROOT / "data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/block_size_ablation_diagnostic.csv")
print(bs.to_string(index=False))
```

The 8-neighbour adjacency correlation is the "leakage risk" number: how
correlated are positive counts in adjacent blocks. At 10 km blocks it's 0.83
(adjacent blocks are nearly perfectly correlated; bad CV). At 30 km it drops
to 0.46 (acceptable). v3's planned 20 km default sits at 0.71.

```{python}
#| label: block-size-plot
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 4))
axes[0].plot(bs.block_size_m / 1000, bs.adjacency_pos_corr,
             marker="o", color="crimson", linewidth=2)
axes[0].set_xlabel("block size (km)")
axes[0].set_ylabel("8-neighbour adjacency positive correlation")
axes[0].set_title("Adjacency leakage (lower is better)")
axes[0].axhline(0.5, color="gray", linestyle=":", alpha=0.6, label="0.5 acceptable")
axes[0].legend()
axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.25)

axes[1].plot(bs.block_size_m / 1000, bs.n_folds,
             marker="o", color="steelblue", linewidth=2, label="n_folds")
ax2 = axes[1].twinx()
ax2.plot(bs.block_size_m / 1000, bs.blocks_with_zero_pos,
         marker="s", color="darkorange", linewidth=2,
         label="zero-pos blocks (wasted)")
axes[1].set_xlabel("block size (km)")
axes[1].set_ylabel("n_folds", color="steelblue")
ax2.set_ylabel("zero-positive blocks", color="darkorange")
axes[1].set_title("Statistical power trade-off")
axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Recommendation**: Phase F.1 will try 25 km first (adjacency 0.57, 96 folds,
57 wasted zero-pos blocks). If the per-fold AUC variance is high we'll
escalate to 30 km. If the wasted-block ratio worries you we'll back down to
20 km.

## E.5 leakage-risk audit

This audit computes Pearson + Spearman correlation between each suspect
feature and each population's binary label. The audit flags features with
|r| > 0.10 as a leakage warning.

```{python}
#| label: leakage-audit
leak = pd.read_csv(REPO_ROOT / "data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/leakage_risk_audit.csv")
print(leak.to_string(index=False))
```

**Finding**: NO features flagged. **And yet we know from v2 that pit-proximity
leaks** (RF AUC 0.995 vs LightGBM 0.804). What's happening:

- At 0.02% positive rate, the Pearson correlation of any feature with the
  binary label has an upper bound near 0.05 just from the prior. Even a
  perfectly-predictive feature would barely cross 0.10.
- The v2 RF leakage was via PATTERN (cells near pit polygons get high score
  because cells AT pit polygons are positives). Pearson doesn't see this —
  it's not a linear correlation, it's a local-neighborhood structure.

This is a real diagnostic limit. Simple correlation isn't useful for
small-positive leakage detection. The real diagnostics for that are:

- **E.4 SHAP recipe match**: if `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered` dominates
  the top-5 Tertiary features it's leakage even when |r| < 0.10.
- **Held-out generalization**: if Lindgren PP73 secondary (de-duped 500m from
  any pit) scores 0 of 13 in the high tail it's leakage.

v3.5 work: improve the leakage audit by adding nearest-neighbour positive
density per cell as a leakage probe (the kind of structure Pearson misses).

## E.1 calibration validation

Pre/post isotonic comparison on the v2 Tertiary calibrated raster:

```{python}
#| label: calibration
cal = pd.read_csv(REPO_ROOT / "data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/calibration_validation_placer_tertiary.csv")
cal_pre = pd.read_csv(REPO_ROOT / "data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/calibration_validation_placer_tertiary_precal.csv")
print("Post-isotonic calibration validation (v2 Tertiary):")
print(cal.to_string(index=False))
print()
print("Pre-isotonic (raw stacking OOF) calibration:")
print(cal_pre.to_string(index=False))
```

Pre-isotonic ECE was 0.0004; post-isotonic ECE is 0.0001 — the calibrator is
genuinely improving the calibration. v3 will inherit isotonic's edge over
Platt at this positive rate (see E.3).

## E.3 Platt vs isotonic ablation

```{python}
#| label: platt-vs-isotonic
ab = pd.read_csv(REPO_ROOT / "data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/calibration_ablation_placer_tertiary.csv")
print(ab.to_string(index=False))
```

Isotonic wins on Tertiary by 100x lower ECE. Sigmoid's top-decile precision
is identical (both identify the same cells as positives), but isotonic's
probability scale matches observed frequencies more closely. v3 keeps the
existing isotonic-default behavior.

## E.2 capture rates

Added to the per-fold metrics CSV. The model card now reports per-model
mean ± std of capture_at_5pct alongside the existing AUC. Implemented as a
helper in `src/ai_minerals/metrics/bootstrap.py`; 9 unit tests cover edge
cases.

## E.4 SHAP recipe match

Implemented in the rationale script + the validation harness model card.
Will produce real numbers when v3 Phase F.1 retrains both populations
(the v2 estimator joblib has stale litho column names that the recipe-match
helper doesn't recognize cleanly; the v3 joblibs will be clean).

The Tertiary recipe expectations: top-5 must include ≥3 of
{paleochannel_likelihood, distance_to_lode_m,
hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered, tertiary_terrace_likelihood,
lithology_class_*}. The Quaternary recipe expectations: top-5 must include
≥3 of {distance_downstream_from_lode_m, catchment_au_hawkes, spi_band,
ksn, distance_to_lode_m, lithology_class_*}.

If Tertiary's top feature is the unbuffered `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m` we
re-introduce the leakage shortcut and the recipe gate fails. The buffered
variant in v3's TERTIARY_FEATURE_COLUMNS is what we expect.

## What's next (Phase F)

Three steps before ship:

1. **F.0** (new): rebuild the feature parquet with v3 assemble (the new
   features + reclassified labels + refined anchor coordinates land).
2. **F.1**: train both populations with v3 settings. Expect ~12-16 hours
   wall clock (Hawkes refactor savings were marginal as Phase A discovered).
3. **F.2-F.4**: audit suite (uses the Phase E scripts above) → ship/no-ship
   gates → chapter update + deploy.
